coredata.dc:title: The application of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers as innovative photocatalysts for degrading organic pollutants in aquatic environments: A comprehensive review

coredata.dc:description: Nanoparticle-doped geopolymers (NP-GPs) have attracted considerable attention as promising photocatalysts applied for the degradation of organic pollutants under light sources in aqueous environments thanks to their ability to synergistically exploit the unique physicochemical properties of geopolymer systems with the high photocatalytic performance of the added semiconducting agents, offering a sustainable solution for water treatment. This review critically analyzes over 130 works published between 2015 and 2025, exploring various synthesis methods, such as direct mixing, ion exchange, dip-coating, and hydrothermal processing. The impact of these techniques on structural, morphological, and optical properties is examined through advanced characterization methods, including X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning and transmission electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface area analysis, and ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. The mechanisms governing pollutant degradation, including charge separation and reactive oxygen species generation, are discussed in detail. Comparative assessments highlight the influence of synthesis strategies and nanoparticle selection on photocatalytic efficiency. While NPGs demonstrate excellent degradation performance, challenges related to long-term stability, recyclability, and large-scale implementation remain. Addressing these issues through optimized material design, improved synthesis protocols, and environmental impact assessments is essential for advancing their practical applications. Future research should also focus on integrating NP-GPs with hybrid treatment technologies to enhance their performance. Developing cost-effective and scalable NP-GP-based systems represents a promising pathway toward sustainable water purification, contributing to global environmental protection efforts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoparticle-doped geopolymers (NP-GPs) have attracted considerable attention as promising photocatalysts applied for the degradation of organic pollutants under light sources in aqueous environments thanks to their ability to synergistically exploit the unique physicochemical properties of geopolymer systems with the high photocatalytic performance of the added semiconducting agents, offering a sustainable solution for water treatment. This review critically analyzes over 130 works published between 2015 and 2025, exploring various synthesis methods, such as direct mixing, ion exchange, dip-coating, and hydrothermal processing. The impact of these techniques on structural, morphological, and optical properties is examined through advanced characterization methods, including X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning and transmission electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface area analysis, and ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. The mechanisms governing pollutant degradation, including charge separation and reactive oxygen species generation, are discussed in detail. Comparative assessments highlight the influence of synthesis strategies and nanoparticle selection on photocatalytic efficiency. While NPGs demonstrate excellent degradation performance, challenges related to long-term stability, recyclability, and large-scale implementation remain. Addressing these issues through optimized material design, improved synthesis protocols, and environmental impact assessments is essential for advancing their practical applications. Future research should also focus on integrating NP-GPs with hybrid treatment technologies to enhance their performance. Developing cost-effective and scalable NP-GP-based systems represents a promising pathway toward sustainable water purification, contributing to global environmental protection efforts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the water shortage linked to limited resources has become a real threat to humanity as it becomes difficult to meet the growing demand for this vital substance [1,2]. This environmental phenomenon has multiple causes, including global warming, unreasonable water use, etc. However, wastewater pollution is considered the most dangerous, affecting both human health and the ecosystem as a whole [3,4]. Industrial expansion remains one of the most important sources of consumption and pollution of waterbeds through the discharge of several types of pollutants, particularly organic ones such as dyes released by textile factories [5], pesticides released by agricultural processes [6], and antibiotics and analgesics released by pharmaceutical manufacturers [7,8]. Against this background, water treatment has gained increasing prominence as an effective strategy for meeting shortage needs and combating the risk of pollution, thanks to adopting a wide range of chemical (ion exchange, chemical hydrolysis…), physical (adsorption, filtration…), and biological technologies (aerobic and anaerobic treatment) [9–11]. Despite the many methods proposed for wastewater decontamination, their application remains limited in terms of reversibility, pollution reappearance, incomplete elimination of organic pollutants, and others [12,13]. However, photodegradation has proved to be an effective technology with multiple and unique advantages, such as quick break-down rates, room operating conditions, economic cost, safety, and the capacity to produce a green product from an organic pollutant, making its real-world application extremely important [14,15]. Still, the large-scale commercial application of this photo-treatment technology is restricted by the limited availability of high-performance photocatalysts, which need to combine efficiency, robustness, low cost, and responsiveness to visible light for photoreactions [16,17].

In looking for high-performance photocatalytic products, nanoparticle-doped geopolymers have emerged as economic and ecological photocatalysts that combine the advantages of geopolymer engineering with photocatalytic characteristics, making them a promising solution for the high-efficiency treatment of water contaminated by organic compounds [18,19]. When talking about this modern type of photocatalyst, it is necessary to introduce geopolymers, which are a type of semi-crystalline to amorphous inorganic polymers produced in a non-polluting way by a geopolymerization reaction that combines aluminosilicate-rich precursors, whether natural or industrial waste, with highly concentrated alkaline solutions at a temperature not exceeding 100 °C [20,21]. Geopolymers have multiple applications thanks to their unique, multi-faceted physicochemical properties [22]. Their high mechanical, thermal, and chemical resistance has made them pioneers in diverse construction applications [23–25]. Moreover, their distinctive surface and porosity properties and their great capacity to be modified to increase their specific surface area make them equally effective in environmental applications such as adsorption, integration, encapsulation, and immobilization of inorganic and organic contaminants [26–28]. The photodegradation field also includes the application of unmodified geopolymers as catalysts, achieving good results in the degradation of organic pollutants, thanks to certain oxides present in the aluminosilicate precursors (SiO2, Al2O3, Fe2O3, TiO2), which act as photocatalytic agents, stimulating chemical decomposition reactions using solar energy or induced light, thus improving the water treatment process [29]. For example, Saufi et al. studied the efficiency of a perlite-based geopolymer as a photocatalyst in decomposing methylene blue dye in aqueous media. This inorganic polymer was found to have a significant efficiency in degrading the MB organic dye, reaching 97.87 % after four hours under UV radiation [30]. In another study by EL Alouani et al., a fly ash-based geopolymer was developed and applied as a catalyst for removing and degrading RhB dye in wastewater. The authors found that the performance of the inorganic polymer used for RhB degradation was good, with an efficiency of 94.07 % during 8 h under artificial UV light [31]. Additionally, the work performed by Liu et al. showed that the phosphoric acid-based geopolymer (PAG) was effective as a photocatalyst in the degradation of the anionic dye Direct Blue-86 in an aqueous solution [32].

Recently, interest in modifying geopolymers using various methods to replace conventional photocatalysts (e.g., TiO2, ZnO, ZnS) has increased. However, some of these photocatalytic semiconductors have shown several limitations that negatively affect the performance of their applications. The disadvantages that have limited the actual large-scale application of these catalysts are mainly related to rapid photogenerated electron-hole recombination, leading to poor photodegradation of pollutants, low solar energy utilization efficiency due to the catalyst's narrow spectral response band, lack of active sites on its surface, high cost, and so on. The joint effect of these disadvantages has meant that some photocatalysts are not easy to apply in practice [33,34]. Photocatalytic engineering, therefore, requires finding, designing, and modifying efficient photocatalyst materials, hence the idea of developing nanoparticle-doped geopolymers. These innovative photocatalysts could replace conventional geopolymers as a new generation of photocatalytic materials because of their high efficiency in degrading various types of organic pollutants at significant concentrations under optimal operating conditions, including low dosage and faster time by using a natural or artificial light source, which guarantees their low cost in water treatment by photodegradation technology [35,36]. Moreover, geopolymer-based photocatalytic materials present notable environmental and economic benefits compared to conventional materials used in water treatment. Their exceptional durability and resistance to chemical and physical degradation ensure long-term stability, even in challenging conditions [37]. Environmentally, geopolymers are synthesized from industrial by-products and waste materials, reducing resource depletion and promoting sustainable waste management. Their production process requires low energy consumption and fewer hazardous chemicals, which makes them an eco-friendly alternative. Economically, using low-cost raw materials lowers manufacturing expenses, making these materials a viable option for large-scale applications [35]. These photocatalytic materials have been developed by incorporating different types of nanoparticles into the geopolymer matrix, which are generally inorganic by nature in the form of semiconductor oxides such as titanium (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), ferric oxide (Fe2O3), copper oxide (Cu2O), manganese dioxide (MnO2), graphite oxide (GO), and others, featuring a low bandgap of around 2-3.5 eV, making their optical absorption high, thus enhancing photodegradation efficiency [38–40]. Research into photocatalytic geopolymeric materials has not been confined to the selection of added nanoparticles but has also focused on optimizing the photocatalytic properties of these hybrid composites by adjusting several factors, including the type and concentration of the photocatalytic nanoparticles, pH solution, the radiation source's intensity, and its wavelength, as well as surface and porosity features, and so on [41,42]. Optimizing photocatalytic characteristics remains crucial in attaining the highest possible efficiency in removing organic substances and pollutants from aquatic environments [43]. Diversifying the synthesis method has also revealed an important role in improving the efficiency of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers in water treatment using photocatalytic technology [44].

Despite the numerous reviews published about the application of geopolymers, modified or not, as photocatalysts in the treatment of water contaminated by organic compounds, at present, there is no review available concerning the latest achievements on the application of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers as photocatalysts in treating the degradation of various types of organic pollutants. In this review article, a brief overview will be reported, highlighting relevant advances made over the past five years in applying geopolymers modified with photocatalytic materials as a promising solution to the problem of water pollution by various organic substances. The first section presents various synthetic methods to incorporate nanoparticulate materials into the geopolymer matrix. The second section includes the physicochemical properties of photocatalyst-based geopolymers obtained using multiple characterization techniques. The next section focuses on the mechanisms of photocatalytic geopolymer materials in degrading different types of organic pollutants in an aqueous solution. Then, a comparison between different nanoparticle-doped geopolymers in photodegrading various organic pollutants under different operating conditions is presented. Concluding observations and future perspectives are finally presented in the last section of this review.

Different techniques have been adopted for preparing photocatalytic nanoparticle-activated geopolymers, incorporating semiconducting nanoparticles as photocatalytic agents in the geopolymeric matrix. Generally speaking, the synthesis of conventional or modified geopolymers relies on the mixture of two main materials, namely aluminosilicate precursors and a highly alkaline solution. The source of aluminosilicate-rich materials can be natural, such as kaolin and other types of clay, or industrial waste, such as fly ash, granulated blast-furnace slag, and others. Alkaline activation is performed either with high-concentration alkali hydroxide (e.g., NaOH, KOH) alone or with alkali silicates (e.g., Na2SiO3, NaOH) [45]. The geopolymerization reaction between the two principal materials involves three main stages, which occur simultaneously and concurrently. The first step entails dissolving aluminosilicate precursors by high-concentration alkaline attack and forming monomeric silicon and aluminum units. Then comes the polycondensation of these activated units, creating an amorphous gel that combines alumina and silica in a chain-like structure. The final stage concerns solidification, where the gel gradually matures over time, thanks to heat treatment, forming a solid geopolymer system [37].

A photocatalytic agent is frequently added during the mixing phase to ensure homogeneous and complete integration into the geopolymer paste, reflecting the performance of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers in degrading organic pollutants under UV light irradiation. Semiconductor nanoparticles added as catalysts to such systems come in the form of oxides like those present in aluminosilicate precursors and others (TiO2, Fe2O3, ZnO, and so on), as they are efficient in enhancing photocatalytic performance. Carbon by-products such as graphite oxide have also been integrated as electron-accepting photocatalysts in geopolymeric materials to enhance their electrical conductivity and, consequently, their photocatalytic performance [46,47]. Various methods have been designed to incorporate the photocatalyst into the geopolymer matrix to produce nanoparticle-doped geopolymers, including direct mixing, impregnation, hydrothermal, and others.

The schematic presentation given in Fig. 1 summarizes a description of the different steps involved in each of the nanoparticle-doped geopolymer synthesis techniques. All the methods are based on the alkaline activation of the aluminosilicate source as the starting preparation stage.

This straightforward method of synthesizing nanoparticle-doped geopolymers as photocatalysts involves mixing the photocatalyst molecules directly with the aluminosilicate source and alkaline solution. For example, Ettahiri et al. formulated porous photocatalytic geopolymers based on calcined natural clay to photodegrade the cationic organic dye RhB. The synthesis was performed by mixing calcined natural clay as an aluminosilicate source and an alkaline activating solution composed of SiO2 and NaOH plus a desired ratio of TiO2 nanoparticles as a catalyst using electrical agitation for 10 min until a homogeneous gel was obtained. Finally, the obtained material was ground, sieved, and washed of excess soda with distilled water, and then the resulting powder was dried for testing as a photocatalyst in photodegradation [48]. In another work, Maiti et al. formulated by direct mixing fly ash-based geopolymer mortars using different sizes of laboratory-prepared titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles (T30, T50, and T100). Alkaline activator (AA) was prepared by mixing sodium hydroxide (10 M) and Na2SiO3 in a 1:2 ratio and blending with fly ash (FA) and normal sands (S) in FA:S and FA: AA ratios of 1:3 and 1:0.8, respectively, by adding around 5 % TiO2 nanoparticles from FA to the mixture. All geopolymer samples were prepared and hardened at room temperature (25 ± 5 °C) for 28 days [49].

Zhang et al. have developed this innovative technique for synthesizing nanoparticle-doped geopolymers in three stages. This multi-step approach begins with alkaline activation to form a Na+ geopolymer, followed by ion exchange to replace Na+ with NH4 +, and finishing with the impregnation of oxides or metals as catalyst nanoparticles [18]. In one of his recent studies, Chen et al. apply this technique for formulating a CaMoO4/electroconductive geopolymer composite serving as a photocatalyst for BV5 dye degradation. During the alkaline activation step, silicomanganese, NaOH, ECB, and water were mixed for 5 min to form the geopolymer paste. The mixture was poured into a steel mold and cured at 80 °C for 6 h, then stored at room temperature for 18 h. After demolding, the sample was left for hardening at ambient conditions for 27 days, followed by grinding to obtain a Na+ geopolymer with EGC particle sizes ranging from 160 to 315 μm. The ion exchange reaction between Na+ and NH4 + was then undertaken by mixing a quantity of Na+ geopolymer with an aqueous solution of CH3COONH4 at room temperature for 2 h. Next, the sample was filtered, washed with demineralized water, and dried to form the NH4 +-geopolymer sample. The final step concerns impregnation by adding a quantity of NH4 +-geopolymer to an aqueous solution of (NH4)6Mo7O24.4H2O overnight at room temperature. Finally, the sample saturated with (NH4)6Mo7O24.4H2O was oven-dried at 65 °C and heated at 400 °C for 4 h to obtain the CaMoO4/EGC geopolymer photocatalyst [50].

This technique involves coating pre-formed geopolymers with layers of photocatalytic material. Ying Wang et al. synthesized a CW-GBFS-based geopolymer by mixing ground blast furnace slag (GBFS) with construction waste (CW) and activating the mixture with sodium silicate (Na2SiO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH), as presented in Fig.2 . The mixture was stirred, molded, and initially cured for 24 h, followed by further curing at room temperature for 3–7 days. Through experimentation, they identified an optimal composition using 40 wt% CW, which provided a good balance between compressive strength and cost. The influence of different alkali activator moduli on the geopolymer's compressive strength was also examined, leading to the selection of ideal curing conditions. In a related procedure, Ying Wang's team prepared SiO2–TiO2-modified CW-GBFS-based geopolymers by first creating a photocatalytic gel. This gel was made by dissolving KH550 in ethanol and incorporating SiO2, TiO2, NaOH, epoxy resin, and polyamide 651. The geopolymer blocks were immersed in this gel using a dip-coating method and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 8 h. The final products, labeled with varying amounts of SiO2–TiO2, exhibited different levels of photocatalytic activity [51].

Similarly, Chon et al. applied a dip-coating method to prepare a TiO2-modified geopolymer as a photocatalytic geopolymer. Firstly, the geopolymer paste was prepared by mixing fly ash and metakaolin with an alkaline activating liquid for 10 min. It was then cured under ambient conditions (20 °C temperature and 90 % relative humidity) for 14 days. The resulting specimens were removed from the molds, rinsed, and then dried over 7 days. The formulated geopolymer was deep-coated in a gaging mixture containing a system of Ti(C4H9O)4 and HN(CH2CH2OH)2 dissolved in C2H5OH-H2O. Geopolymer-modified TiO2 composites were oven-dried at 50 °C for 30 min before cooling at ambient temperature. This process was followed multiple times to produce multilayered films. Geopolymer-coated TiO2 films were annealed at various temperatures between 500 °C and 800 °C for 60 min [52].

This method synthesizes photocatalytic geopolymer nanocomposites by combining precursors in an autoclave under controlled temperature and pressure conditions. For example, Changshuai Chen synthesized a series of TiO2-loaded geopolymer composites using a hydrothermal technique. Different geopolymers, denoted as G1, G2, and G3, were employed as support materials. Initially, 0.5 g of each geopolymer was mixed with 9.56 mL of titanium isopropoxide solution. The mixture was then combined with 50 mL of deionized water to create a reaction medium, followed by 2 h of sonication to ensure thorough mixing. The suspension was subsequently subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, filtered, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the dried powder was calcined at 700 °C for 3 h to produce the final TiO2-geopolymer composites, labeled as G1@TiO2, G2@TiO2, and G3@TiO2, depending on the specific geopolymer used [53]. There has also been considerable interest in ZnO as an excellent photocatalytic agent, thanks to its large band gap and high absorption of UV light. In this respect, Sarkar and colleagues have employed a hydrothermal method to synthesize a ZnO/LDSGP-based geopolymer photocatalyst. After sonication in ethanol, the slag was added to a Zn(NO₃)₂ solution. Subsequently, an oxalic-acid solution was incorporated drop-by-drop into the resulting mixture and placed in an autoclave at 115 °C for 12 h. The final blend was filtrated and rinsed with purified water and ethanol. Afterward, the specimen was oven-dried at 90 °C for 12 h. Finally, the blended powder of the precipitate was thermally treated at 500 °C for 12 h [54].

Table 1 lists the various techniques mentioned above that a group of researchers has used to incorporate semiconducting nanoparticles into a geopolymer matrix to synthesize nanoparticle-doped geopolymers as photocatalysts, together with their catalytic activities for photodegrading organic pollutants under different testing conditions. Each of these methods presents distinct advantages and limitations that influence photocatalytic efficiency and material stability. Among them, direct mixing is the simplest and most cost-effective, allowing even distribution of photocatalytic agents, which makes it suitable for large-scale applications. However, it suffers from nanoparticle aggregation, limiting surface activity and reducing degradation efficiency. Despite this, it has demonstrated high performance in pollutant removal, such as methylene blue and rhodamine B, achieving degradation rates above 95 % under UV light [48,49]. In contrast, the alkaline activation-ion exchange-impregnation approach enhances the integration of metal oxides, resulting in improved nanoparticle dispersion and stability. This method has shown exceptional performance, particularly with Mn2+ and CuO doping, reaching complete DSB 5B dye degradation [55]. The presence of CaMoO4 in geopolymers also contributes to the nearly total removal of BV5 dye within 90 min [50]. However, these benefits come at the cost of increased processing complexity, requiring multiple preparation steps and leading to possible structural modifications in the geopolymer framework. Dip-coating, another technique, is particularly effective in forming uniform, thin films of photocatalysts, enhancing light absorption and surface interaction. Although geopolymers synthesized using this method exhibit moderate degradation efficiency, prolonged irradiation is often required to achieve significant pollutant removal. For instance, a TiO2-coated geopolymer film achieved 70.5 % methylene blue degradation after 480 min under UV light [52]. While beneficial for surface functionalization, dip-coating requires multiple applications to maintain efficiency, and coating durability remains a challenge, especially in long-term use. The hydrothermal technique, known for producing highly crystalline and phase-pure nanostructures, significantly enhances the photocatalytic activity of geopolymers. This method has demonstrated outstanding efficiency, with ZnO-doped geopolymers removing up to 96.97 % of Congo red dye in just 60 min under compact fluorescent lamp irradiation [54]. The resulting well-defined nanostructures from this process improve charge separation and light absorption, leading to superior photocatalytic performance. However, the high temperature and pressure conditions required make it a power-intensive method during an extended duration, resulting in high costs, which makes it less practical for large-scale applications.

Overall, each synthesis method presents a trade-off between simplicity, efficiency, and processing complexity. Direct mixing remains the most accessible approach but struggles with nanoparticle stability, whereas ion exchange and impregnation enhance photocatalytic performance at the cost of increased preparation steps. Dip-coating improves surface functionality but requires multiple applications for sustained efficiency, while the hydrothermal method achieves superior material properties but demands greater energy input. The choice of synthesis technique ultimately depends on the specific application requirements, balancing photocatalytic efficiency, structural integrity, and scalability for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the photocatalytic nanoparticle-doped geopolymers
ce:section.ce:para: Various physicochemical characterization techniques have been adopted to analyze geopolymers' structural, morphological, textural, and physicochemical properties before and after doping with semiconducting nanoparticles, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), BET/BJH (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller/Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) analysis, scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM/EDS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM).

XRD is an important technique in identifying the crystalline nature and mineralogical composition of nanoparticle-modified geopolymeric employed as photocatalysts. The information provided by the resulting diffraction patterns, such as the appearance, disappearance, or displacement of crystalline peaks and the like, helps to understand certain physicochemical properties that may explain the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the doped material. Guzmán-Carrillo et al. applied XRD to analyze the mineralogical composition of a new geopolymer photocatalyst based on metal slag (SL) and metakaolin (MK) and doped with zinc oxide nanoparticles to remove MB from water [56]. Fig. 3 reveals the XRD result of the ZnO nanoparticles-doped geopolymer based on metakaolin (without ZnO (mkW), with nanorods (mkR) and nanospheres (mkS) without ZnO, and with nanorods (mkRf) and nanospheres (mkSf) with ZnO) and metal slag (without ZnO (slW), with nanorods (slR) and nanospheres (slS) without ZnO, and with nanorods (slRf) and nanospheres (slSf) with ZnO). It was observed that MK has a significant peak at 2θ values from 20° to 30° (Fig. 3a), which is attributed to the raw amorphous MK element with crystalline phases like α-quartz and cristobalite. The pastes exhibited a slight displacement of the halo to higher 2θ ranges, confirming the formation of the N-A-S-H geopolymer gel amorphous phase.

The doped pastes detected the presence of additional peaks of ZnO nanoparticles, demonstrating that ZnO is embedded in the geopolymer pastes. Fig. 3b shows the XRD spectra of doped geopolymer based on metal slag. A sharp peak is found at 2θ values between 38° and 40°, identified as a main constituent of amorphous slag, as well as crystalline phases of calcite and merwinite. The modification of peaks typical of paste models suggests the precipitation of the geopolymer gel C-A-S-H with the formation of a hydrocalumite crystalline phase. The doping of pastes with ZnO nanoparticles reveals sharper peaks, indicating their reactivity.

In another search, the crystalline phase of raw and doped geopolymer-based photocatalysts has been described by XRD analysis to the degradation of MB under UV light [57]. Fig. 4 illustrates the XRD spectra of the various raw materials used in geopolymer synthesis and the variable contents of cadmium sulfide (CdS) nanoparticles. The XRD spectra of the various raw materials recorded an amorphous phase of geopolymer at the 30° peak (Fig. 4a). The hexagonal CdS crystalline formation was detected in the CdS pure nanoparticle crystal planes (203), (002), (102), (103), (112), (100), (101), (110), and (201), such that the peaks of the CdS pure nanoparticles are consistent with the novel peaks of the CdS-loaded geopolymers. The diffraction peaks of Fe2O3 and CdS in composite geopolymers designate that the geopolymerisation process preserved the crystalline planes of the semiconductor materials, meaning that the charged geopolymer has visible photocatalysis potential. DWTR-loaded samples exhibited no Fe2O3 diffraction peaks relative to the strong Fe2O3 peaks in GP-Fe2O3-CdS and GP-Fe2O3, suggesting that Fe2O3 is found in the amorphous phase of DWTR. Fig. 4b reveals that CdS peaks are more visible when the CdS amount is higher, whereas no significant peaks are measured in the doped geopolymer with 2 % of CdS owing to the low content of CdS crystals.

FTIR spectroscopy represents one of the most widely used techniques for identifying the main functional groups forming nanoparticle-doped geopolymer photocatalysts. This technique also provides insight into the various types of chemical bonds present in a substance by measuring the infrared radiation absorption of the sample being analyzed. FTIR analysis detected several major functional groups characteristic of unmodified and photocatalyst-modified geopolymers. Table 2 records the various functional groups detected by FTIR in geopolymeric materials before and after doping with photocatalytic semiconductors.

These groups mainly include vibrational asymmetrical stretching of Si-O-T (tetrahedral Al or Si), asymmetrical stretching of Si-O-Si and Si-O-Al, stretching of OH, bending of H-O-H, and others. Hydroxyl groups can contribute to the photocatalytic activity of unmodified geopolymer. After incorporating semiconductor nanoparticles into geopolymer matrices, other characteristic M-O stretching bands associated with the type of metal oxide incorporated into the material appear, such as TiO2, ZnO, NiO, CdS, MnO, Cu2O, etc., which are frequently employed as photocatalytic agents.

For example, Kaya-Ozkiper et al. examined the FTIR patterns of geopolymer (RMGP) synthesis by metakaolin and red mud as raw materials and doped with anatase (TiO2) to determine its functional components for use in the photodegradation of MB under dark and visible light irradiation [65]. Fig. 5 confirms the formation of the geopolymerization in the wavelengths between 900 cm-1 and 1200 cm-1. The absorption peaks located at 870 cm-1 reflect the bending vibrations of the Si-OH bond. Asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si bonds corresponding to unreacted metakaolin were observed at 1065 cm-1. The band at 970 cm-1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-T bonds (T = Al or Si), which reveal the formation of a new geopolymer structure. The formation of Ti-O-Ti bonds explains the appearance of the band at 670 cm-1, demonstrating the existence of anatase in the geopolymer.

In addition, He et al. analyzed the FTIR of carbon-based porous geopolymer precursor (rpsBC-GPs) extracted from paper-making sludge using K+ methods for the degradation of tetracycline antibiotics under UV light [66]. The FTIR spectra of rpsBC-GPs are proved in Fig. 6 . The stretching and bending vibrations of the O-H are achieved at 3434 cm-1 and 1642 cm-1, respectively, associating to the abundance of Si(OH)4- and Al(OH)4- ions present in the geopolymer. The O-C-O bond vibrations of CO3 2- ions appeared at 1428 cm-1. When the K+ concentration is increased, the calcite crystals are more clearly exposed. It was indicated that the geopolymer is characterized by a maximum asymmetric starching vibration band of T-O-Si (T: Si or Al) at 1022 cm-1; this vibration is displaced from 1022 cm-1 to 1006 cm-1 following the insertion of K+. At 1006 cm-1, the expansion vibration of rpsBC-GPs2 was the strongest compared to the others, reflecting the fact that the incorporation of excessive K+ destructed the Si-O-Al bond and expanded the C network. The formation of Metal-O bonds of iron or calcium is attained at 678 cm-1, and the appearance of organics on the rpsBC surface is discovered by the bending vibration of C-H at 871.5 cm-1.

DRS spectroscopy provides a commonly employed analytical technique for characterizing photocatalytic materials, including nanoparticle-based geopolymers. It measures the optical diffuse reflectivity of the analyzed material depending on the incident light wavelength. This technique plays an important role in describing the photocatalytic performance of photocatalytic products, providing information on optical properties such as absorption behavior, band gap energy, and the electronic band structure of the material concerned.

The band-gap energy of a photocatalytic refers to the energy difference between its valence band and conduction band, which is essential for determining its ability to absorb light and facilitate photodegradation reactions. The band-gap energy values of various photocatalytic nanoparticle-doped geopolymers are exposed in Table 3 . A photon with an energy greater than or equal to the band gap energy of the nanoparticle-doped photocatalytic geopolymers excited electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, creating electron-hole pairs that lead to chemical reactions. The band gap energy influences the performance of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers. When band gap energy values are high, the displacement of electrons and their participation in the reaction become very limited and reduce overall efficiency. The band-gap energy must be carefully adjusted to ensure that the nanoparticle-doped geopolymers absorb the right amount of light and perform efficiently in the photodegradation of organic pollutants.

Sarkar et al. investigated the band gap and optical sensitivity of a new geopolymer doped by ZnO nanoparticles using Linz-Donawitz slag-based geopolymer (ZnO/LDSGP) for the removal of Congo red under UV light, as presented in Fig. 7 [73]. An absorption band of ZnO/LDSGP is observed in the wavelength range of 390–450 nm (Fig. 7), revealing the effect of visible light on the ZnO/LDSGP. The Tauc plot was used to determine the band gap energy of ZnO/LDSGP. The gap energy of ZnO/LDSGP is lower, around 2.95 eV, compared to that of commercial ZnO nanoparticles (E g = 3.37 eV), demonstrating the photo-activity performance of ZnO/LDSGP relative to commercial ZnO nanoparticles, which positively influences the charge transfer mechanism and the separation of photo-generated electrons.

Nevertheless, Liu et al. developed a geopolymer based on reduced graphene oxide with different quantities doped with zinc oxide nanoparticles (rGO@ZnO/GP) as a photocatalyst for eliminating MB using UV light [74]. The DRS spectroscopy is considered to verify the stability and quality of rGO@ZnO/GP and is illustrated in Fig. 8 . The findings suggest that the samples have similar optical properties. The absorption band of rGO@ZnO/GP exhibited a considerable red shift after coating with different amounts of rGO@ZnO in GP (Fig. 8a), revealing that this coating increased the photocatalytic activity.

The Kubelka-Munk function plot was used to estimate the band gaps of the samples. GP's energy band is greater, showing GP's low photocatalytic activity (Fig. 8a), while the band gap of the 56 %rGO@ZnO/GP sample is lower than that of the other samples at around 3.12 eV. The ZnO can be identified as an active photocatalytic semiconductor material, while the band gaps of rGO@ZnO/GP are comparable.

The BET/BJH method represents a physicochemical analytical process that has been widely used for determining the surface and porosity properties of photocatalytic geopolymeric materials before and after doping with nanoparticles. The data relating to the specific surface area can be determined by BET analysis, while everything concerning the size and dimensional distribution of pores is provided by BJH analysis.

Table 4 illustrates the BET/BJH findings of several nanoparticle-doped geopolymer photocatalysts. In certain instances, incorporating high-surface-area semiconducting nanoparticles, such as TiO2, into the geopolymer matrix can increase the specific surface area of the resulting photocatalytic geopolymers. This increase in surface area may be due to the adhesion of small semiconducting particles onto the geopolymer surface, which could improve photocatalytic performance.

Alternatively, in other cases, the specific surface area of modified geopolymers may decrease after doping nanoparticles. This reduction may be attributed to the aggregation of semiconductor particles, leading to the development of photocatalytic geopolymers with low specific surface areas. The improvement in surface properties, and hence photocatalytic performance, of semiconductor-doped geopolymers depends mainly on the type and quantity of catalyst incorporated into the geopolymer matrix.

Peturai et al. investigated the physical properties using the BET/BHJ technique of two samples of porous geopolymer synthesis based on metakaolin granules and doped with different contents of titanium dioxide (TiO2-GP) and iron oxide (Fe2O3-GP) as photocatalysts for the elimination of the imidacloprid insecticide using UV irradiation [84]. The pore volume and pore size distribution of TiO2-GP and Fe2O3-GP are shown in Fig. 9 . According to the results, the specific surface areas of 14 % Fe2O3-GP and 20 % Fe2O3-GP are around 19.680 m2/g and 17.121 m2/g with average pore sizes of 18.641 nm and 20.037 nm, and the total pore volumes are 0.105 cm3/g and 0.112 cm3/g, respectively, confirming that Fe2O3-GP have a large surface area with significant pore sizes, making them suitable as photocatalysts.

As a result, 14 % TiO2-GP and 7 % TiO2-GP were characterized, respectively, by a surface area between 12.813 m2/g and 18.914 m2/g and a pore size around 14.695 nm and 20.553 nm with a pore volume of 0.053 cm3/g and 0.115 cm3/g, suggesting that the large and medium pores of the geopolymer were occupied by TiO2 nanoparticles, forming a high-density structure that allows imidacloprid molecules to localize in the reactive sites. The pore size and volume of GP are influenced by the deposition of TiO2 in GP, such that increasing TiO2 content decreases pore size and volume. However, the increase in Fe2O3 had no slight impact on pore size and volume, indicating a negligible influence of Fe2O3 amount on the characteristics of pores in porous GP.

However, hierarchical porous geopolymers (PGs) were prepared using fly ash as raw material, the aluminum PGs were treated with hydrochloric acid and doped with cobalt oxide (Co3O4/PGs) to improve their performance in the photodegradation of direct acid-resistant Bordeaux [85]. The specific surface area of PGs and Co3O4/PGs is analyzed by the BET/BHJ method and presented in Fig. 10 . A hysteresis buckle in all samples is found around the relative pressure interval of 0.40 to 0.98, confirming a mesopore structure (Fig. 10a). It was reported that the acid-treated PGs curve presented a fast increase at low pressure (<0.10) compared to the original PGs, showing a type I isotherm, reflecting that the PGs has a microporous structure. The efficacy of the acid treatment is demonstrated in modifying the material's pore structure. Through an increase in acid concentration, the PGs isotherm changed progressively, developing into a mixture of type I and type IV, suggesting that the PGs have a richer microporous structure, which is ascribed to eliminating aluminum from the original geopolymer structure by HA. The surface area of PGs increased from 88.12 m²/g to 265.46 m²/g, which is twice the surface area of the original HG geopolymers, and then a significant decrease was observed as the acid treatment concentration increased (Fig. 10b).

The same finding was observed for the total pore volume, where the volume increased from 0.1477 cm³/g to 0.1696 cm³/g and then decreased to 0.1331 cm³/g as the concentration of HCl used increased. In terms of pore size, two peaks at 1.2 nm and 9.3 nm were assigned to the original HG geopolymer sample, and the pore sizes localized at 1.2 nm and 2.7 nm were attached to the acid-treated PGs. The specific surface area of Co3O4/PGs is 184.12 m²/g, with the pore size and volume being around 2.89 nm and 0.1331 cm³/g, respectively. A reduction in the zone of micropores and the surface area of Co3O4/PGs is detected, while the area of mesopores increased significantly, meaning that the Co3O4 is localized in the micropores of PGs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3.5 Scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: SEM/EDS is a microscopy technique that enables the observation of the chemical composition and surface structure of samples at high magnifications, providing detailed information on the size, shape, and distribution of particles. This instrument has been widely applied to the micromorphological and semi-quantitative analysis of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers for studying the elements' distribution and morphology of photocatalytic nanoparticles and their loading degree in the geopolymeric matrix. It can likewise be utilized to assess the surface texture, porosity, and composition of the modified geopolymer surface. For instance, Wenjing et al. synthesized novel Mn-doped metakaolin-based geopolymer microspheres (Mn-MGPs) at varying temperatures (150, 180, and 210 °C) to be used in the photodegradation of malachite green and tetracycline applying visible light irradiation [86]. SEM/EDS analysis was employed to determine the chemical composition and the surface morphology of the Mn-MGPs, as shown in Fig. 11 . Mn-MGPs have a hollow microsphere structure (Fig. 11a and b). The EDS results of Mn-MGPs confirmed a homogeneous distribution of the elements external to the pores (Fig. 11c). Al and Si elements are the principal constituents and the main particles in the N-A-S-H gel. At 150 °C and 180 °C, a staged arrangement of polygonal nanosheets was obtained, while at 210 °C, a dense radial stem pattern was achieved (Fig. 11 d-f). A porous structure was established on the surface of the spheres, with Mn-MGPs at 210 °C having slightly more pores and Mn-MGPs at 150 °C having bigger pores. The study concluded from the SEM/EDS analysis that Mn-MGPs particles possess a significant porous structure that increases with higher temperatures, forming a three-dimensional gel network structure.

The work of Linqiao et al. studied the surface morphology and the elements distribution of slag-based geopolymer microspheres (S@GMs) before and after the removal of tetracycline under UV light [87]. Fig. 12 shows the SEM/EDS analysis results of S@GMs before and after the photodegradation of tetracycline. It was found that the surface of S@GMs did not change before and after the photodegradation process, suggesting the stability of S@GMs with a homogeneous structure and a porous surface. Evaluation of the S@GM surface composition revealed a slight presence of silicon, aluminum, oxygen, and magnesium before and after the photodegradation, while a significant reduction was recorded in the calcium content from 17 % to 14.29 % during the photodegradation of tetracycline. This confirms the interaction of calcium ions with tetracycline, providing a positive charge and activating the production of free radicals.

This analysis delivers the observation of microstructures on a nanometric scale, providing high-resolution micrographs for determining nanoparticle size, shape, and distribution in a given material. Photocatalytic geopolymers are among the materials that TEM has analyzed to offer valuable nanoscale morphological information concerning the crystal morphology, size, and shape of doped photocatalytic particles and their distribution in the resulting geopolymer matrix. Ji et al. performed TEM analysis using electron wavelengths to examine the morphology, size, and shape of hierarchical porous geopolymer doped by CdS (HPG) based on waste materials for the photodegradation of MB under visible light irradiation, as revealed in Fig. 13 [88]. An amorphous structure of the geopolymer is enclosed by CdS crystalline with a lattice distance of 0.33 nm, revealing a hexagonal shape of the CdS particles.

Falah et al. developed a novel geopolymer using Cu2O/TiO2 nanoparticles modified with cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a photocatalyst to remove MB under UV radiation [89]. The TEM microscopy was used to identify the microstructure of Cu2O/TiO2-CTAB Gp and is presented in Fig. 14 . The 10 % Cu2O/TiO2-CTAB Gp provides the effective doping of Cu2O/TiO2 nano-heterostructures into Cu2O/TiO2-CTAB Gp (Fig. 14a and c). A micrograph at high resolution of a TiO2 nanoparticle was detected, showing the associated fast Fourier transform inset (FFT), revealing that this TiO2 nanoparticle grew in a (121) direction (Fig. 14b). The 0.23 nm lattice spacings are measured directly from the diffraction pattern. The d-spacings associated with the well-defined diffraction rings of the selected zone are in accordance with the TiO2 tetragonal phase at the (101) and (211) planes and the cubic phase Cu2O in the (111) and (200) planes (Fig. 14d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General mechanism of degrading aquatic organic pollutants by Nanoparticle-doped geopolymers
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysts based on nanoparticle-doped geopolymers are extensively used to purify organic contaminants in the aquatic ecosystem. Many researchers have turned to improve the optical properties of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers as photocatalytic, exploiting their many physicochemical features, including superior conductivity, adequate visible light absorption, large specific surface area, reduced re-combination of light-induced convection vectors, high-porosity, tunable pore-size, and excellent stability [90–92]. The research conducted by Benkhirat et al. suggests the manufacture of a new ZnO-doped geopolymer foam as a phenol photocatalyst [93]. The photocatalyst based on ZnO-doped geopolymer is synthesized according to the SILAR method involving impregnation of an alveolar-structure substrate into the geopolymer gel in two steps, the first consists of the formulation of a geopolymer paste, which includes creating a homogeneous gel from metakaolin, while the second uses the solidification process to provide a porous structure of the geopolymer. The temperature variation on the ZnO-doped geopolymer pore network limits the pore volume and size. The photodegradation process is achieved under UV light (UV lamp 365 nm and 104 W/m2) with a phenolic solution of 200 mL. A high efficiency is detected for the degradation of 10 mg/L phenol in a basic medium (pH = 9) during 4 h with a precursor concentration of 0.8 M doped ZnO in geopolymer and 60 quenching cycles. Besides, an autonomous solar prototype reactor equipped with a TiO2-doped geopolymer photocatalyst is introduced as an alternative process for the photodegradation of paracetamol (PCT) under UV radiation [94]. The reactor is developed as a modular system to combine the oxidation and the purification of solar disinfection using photocatalysts. TiO2 was dispersed by coating the surface of the geopolymer and applying it to aluminum U-profiles bonded by polymer-based connecting elements using 3D-printed filaments. The photodegradation of PCT is produced only by oxidizing the TiO2-doped geopolymer photocatalyst. The reactor identifies a high photodegradation capacity of around 75.1 % of PCT 10 mg/L for 210 h in a practically neutral solution (pH between 6 and 7). It was concluded that the photodegradation of PCT is influenced by the increase in the illuminated area and contact time.

The nanoparticle-doped geopolymer photocatalyst can be formed using semiconductors, such as MoO3, ZnO, TiO2, WO3, CdS, Fe2O3, Cu2O, CeO2, and ZrO2. TiO2 and ZnS are regarded as the most popular semiconductor nanoparticles incorporated into the geopolymer matrix as photocatalysts due to their numerous benefits, including cost-effectiveness, stability, abundance, nontoxicity, and the capacity to produce strong oxidative species [95,96]. In the same manner, a new eco-friendly photocatalyst porous geopolymer composite membrane (PGCM) has been synthesized based on TiO2 for removing antibiotics from hospital effluents [97]. PGCM has two layers, a porous layer bonded to a mesoporous dense layer of 700 μm embedded by TiO2 with varying content from 0 to 10 wt%. The reduction of open porosity, total pore volume, and average pore size of the dense layer is detected when the concentration of TiO2 increases in the geopolymer matrix, decreasing water permeability. PGCM with 10 % TiO2 provides a high efficiency for the degradation of norfloxacin (NOR), tetracycline (TET), trimethoprim (TRI), amoxicillin (AMX), sulfamethoxazole (SUL), and ciprofloxacin (CIP) from a hospital effluent under UV light using a 300 W lamp. The photodegradation of antibiotics is achieved in 2 h with a thickness of 5 mm and a diameter of 70 mm of PGCM (25 g) submerged in 1 L of hospital effluent comprising 66 μg/L of NOR, 893 μg/L of TET, 121 μg/L of TRI, 13 μg/L of AMX, 51 μg/L of SUL, and 24 μg/L of CIP at neutral pH. It was revealed that PGCM with 10 % TiO2 exhibited significant effectiveness for photodegradation of NOR (75 %) and CIP (73 %) relative to the other antibiotics TRI (46 %), SUL (38 %), TET (69 %), and AMX (63 %), which may be justified by the high absorbability and consequent degradability of PGCM via NOR and CIP. According to the findings, the performance of antibiotic photodegradation is improved by increasing the amount of TiO2 coating in PGCM. Nevertheless, Desta Shumuye et al. investigated the influence of ZnO nanoparticles on the microstructural behavior of engineered geopolymer composites (EGCs) for the photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) [98]. The ultrasonic method is applied for the better dispersion of ZnO with high contents, forming a higher quality of agglomeration due to the large specific surface area of the nanoparticles (88.890 m2/kg), with consequent thickening of the matrix EGCs. The self-cleaning of 5 % and 10 % nanodoped ZnO in EGCs demonstrates an important ability to degrade 0.4 μg/mL RhB under UV irradiation using lamps with wavelengths of 400 μm within 15 days. The data indicate that the photodegradation of RhB increases with increasing concentration of ZnO nanoparticles doped in EGCs.

The photocatalyst accelerates the decomposition of organic molecules by producing hydroxyl radicals in the presence of a source of ultraviolet irradiation, which can be natural (sunlight) or artificial (lamp-light) [99,100]. For example, an amorphous geopolymer is doped with TiO2 using two coating methods, simple (roller coating) and durable (spray coating), for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) [101]. Roller coating was fast with large, flat surfaces, whereas spray coating was relatively easy for industrial scale and can coating complex substrates. A batch reactor is evaluated for the photodegradation of MB by TiO2-doped geopolymer under UV light using a 215 W lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. The initial concentration of 10 μmol/L MB can be degraded to 50 % yield in 5 h using TiO2-doped geopolymer. The detection of significant concentrations of TiO₂ on the coating surface confirms its distribution in the geopolymer framework, which justifies the enhanced capacity for the photocatalytic activity of TiO2-doped geopolymer in the photodegradation of MB. Similar research was controlled by Bora et al. for the photodegradation of MB using ZnO-doped granulated blast furnace slag geopolymer (GGBFS) under sunlight [102]. ZnO-GGBFS is distinguished by a large surface area of around 40.3 m2/g with a pore size of 290.793 Å and a pore volume of 0.307 cm3/g, while the band gap value is estimated at 3.16 eV. Based on the findings, 40 mg/L of MB can be decomposed easily with a rate of 95 % during 1 h using a dose of 1 g/L of ZnO-GGBFS sunlight. The ZnO nanoparticle is impregnated in GGBFS, forming a significant surface and porosity. Increasing the value of the irradiated energy compared to the brightness band allows electrons to move to the conduction band, producing holes in the valence band.

The mechanism of photocatalysis using doped geopolymer systems can be considered external since it relies on the presence of the semiconducting nanocomposites integrated into their matrix for photodegrading organic pollutants in aquatic media. By exposing photocatalytic geopolymers to UV irradiations with photon energy higher than or equivalent to the band gap of the modified catalyst (hυ ≥ Eg), the available semiconducting nanoparticles interact with the absorbed light and become excited [103]. This phenomenon encourages the photoelectron transition from the occupied valence band (VB) to the empty conduction band (CB), resulting in the photogeneration of electron/hole pairs (e-/h+) with the positively charged hole (h+) occupying the VB and the electron (e-) the CB, as demonstrated by Eq. (1) [104]. These newly formed free e-/h+ pairs are generally known as “charge holders” [105].

(2) [106]. The holes h+ also react with pollutant molecules adsorbed on the surface following the reaction shown in Eq. (3). These species formed by an oxidation reaction are very potent oxidizing agents capable of attacking and breaking down adsorbed organic pollutants in aqueous systems [107].

All these oxidizing sorts are very reactive and can oxidize several organic pollutants to green products (H2O and CO2), as shown in Eq. (8) [112], or evolve “functional groups” that raise their solubility, enabling them to be removed by washing [113].

The general suggestion concerning the photodegradation mechanism of organic compounds by nanoparticle-doped geopolymer photocatalysts (NP-GPs) is summarized schematically in Fig. 15 .

Particularly, organic dyes, one of the common organic pollutants destined for photocatalytic testing, have conjunctive properties sufficient for absorbing visible light. Therefore, they are destroyed during interaction with photocatalysts when excited by a visible photon from the fundamental state to the tri-excited state with a wavelength above 400 nm [114]. The tri-excited state inserts an electron (e-) into the conduction band of the photocatalytic geopolymer. Then, it transforms into a semi-oxidizing “radial cation” and forms superoxide anion radicals (
O 2 −
●) and hydroxyl radicals (HO●) [115]. These hydroxyl radicals (HO●) are primarily associated with oxidizing the organic composites, in addition to the valence-band photogenerated positive holes and the conduction-band photogenerated electrons, which are also involved in oxidizing and reducing the organic pollutants, respectively [116]. However, it is important to point out that these reaction processes only involve a restricted number of organic compounds, like dyes, capable of absorbing visible light [117]. The photocatalytic performance of the target semiconducting materials for doping the geopolymer matrix relies on both the photocatalyst's capacity for producing electrons and holes and generating active free radicals [118]. Consequently, the photocatalyst's specific surface area, microstructure, and bandgap properties represent the significant properties that influence its effectiveness [119].

Luévano‑Hipólito et al. reported the self-cleaning of photocatalytic geopolymers (PG) created from industrial wastes like fly ash and slag, doped by TiO2 nanoparticles for the photodegradation of reactive black (RB5) under solar irradiation [120]. The synthesis of PG is improved by Taguchi L9 orthogonal design by varying the temperature and the contents of fly ash and TiO2 to achieve greater self-cleaning efficiency. The slag activation is dominated by zeolite X and calcium aluminosilicate hydrate. According to the characterization results, GP is formed only from slag (without fly ash) at 300 °C with 3 % TiO2 and has a high geopolymer prototype. The gap energy of GP-3 % TiO₂ is attracted to 3.1 eV, equivalent to an absorption band of 400 nm combined with the energy band gap of the anatase phase, whereas its specific surface area is achieved at around 9.6 m²/g, with the pore size and volume being 1.6 nm and 0.040 cm3/g, respectively. A degradation of 88.4 % of RB5 with a concentration of 50 ppm is registered in 3 days under sunlight. The self-cleaning mechanism of GP-3 % TiO2 for RB5 is commenced by the absorption of light by TiO2 to favor the electron transfer from VB to CB, allowing the formation of the photogenerated pair on the surface of TiO2. The reaction between OH- or H2O and h+ in VB leads to the hydroxyl radical HO●. The superoxide radical anions (
O 2 −
●) are produced by the excitation of the electrons in CB reacting with the O2 in the air and subsequently combining with the OH- to form new HO● radicals, causing the oxidation of RB5 and its decomposition on the surface of GP-3 % TiO₂. The interaction of TiO2 nanoparticles with the GP phases provides supplementary active sites for the self-cleaning reaction of RB5 under sunlight.

The selection of semiconductors for their incorporation into the geopolymer matrix depends on several criteria, including the necessity of its having a unique electronic structure ( i.e., filled valence band and free conduction band), sufficiently low band gaps for the electron to be excited in the conduction band by sunlight, capable of making efficient use of visible radiation or near-ultraviolet radiations (λ > 400 nm); they must also be chemically and biologically inactive, optically stable, cost-effective and non-poisonous [121,122]. In response to these criteria, several semiconducting oxides have been used for this purpose, demonstrating their effectiveness in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers. As previously shown in Table 3, TiO2 (n-type) and ZnO (n-type) have a wide bandgap of approximately 3.03 eV and 3.37 eV, respectively, while TiO2 ensures that only 5 % of incident sunlight falls within a wavelength suitable for photoexcitation, whereas ZnO facilitates activation under sunlight through good coupling to the visible light beam [123,124].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of nanoparticle-doped geopolymer photocatalysts’ efficiencies in degrading different types of organic pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers in photodegrading organic contaminants varies significantly based on the type of pollutant, catalyst properties, synthesis method, operational conditions, and others. Table 5 provides a detailed comparative analysis of various nanoparticle-doped geopolymer photocatalysts, highlighting their degradation efficiency across dyes, pesticides, and antibiotics, the common organic pollutants present in different aquatic environments.

Nanoparticle-doped geopolymers have been extensively studied for degrading dye pollutants such as Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), Congo Red (CR), and others. For example, Ettahiri et al. studied incorporating semiconducting TiO2 nanoparticles as photocatalysts in a geopolymer matrix to develop a new photo-catalytic TiO2/geopolymer effective in degrading aqueous RhB dye. Geopolymers based on heat-treated natural clay and doped with TiO2 were synthesized by a simple direct mixing method, enabling the successful loading of TiO2 nanoparticles into the porous geopolymer surface. Photodegradation results indicated that the 1.0 g/L dose of geopolymer photocatalyst containing 5 % TiO2 achieved the best RhB dye degradation efficiency of 97 % in aqueous solution under UV light in 90 min. Fig. 16 reveals that under UV illumination, titanium dioxide in GPT1 having a band gap of 3.17 eV generated electron-hole pairs and induced hydroxyl radicals (HO●) and active oxygen radicals (
O 2 −
●), resulting in the oxidation of RhB molecules previously adsorbed on the geopolymer surface into H2O and CO2 molecules and other green products [48]. In another study, Chen et al. investigated a hydrothermally synthesized geopolymer-TiO2 composite, achieving 94 % RhB degradation under similar conditions, although it required a longer reaction time of 120 min [53]. This suggests that synthesis methods influence the structural and catalytic properties of geopolymers.

Similarly, as reported by Jaramillo-Fierro et al., a metakaolin-based geopolymer matrix was loaded with a ZnTiO3/TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst (geopolymer/ZnTiO3/TiO2) to enhance its efficiency in degrading methylene blue (MB) dye by UV light. The photocatalytic geopolymer based on MK:ZnTiO3/TiO2 with a ratio of 7:3 showed an excellent MB removal rate of 93 % at an initial concentration of 20 mg/L for 150 min using only 0.2 g/L photocatalyst in a neutral pH medium (7). After the MB molecules adsorption onto the GTA surface, photocatalytic processes start by irradiating the reaction medium with UV light, rendering the geopolymer photocatalyst capable of degrading the dye pollutant due to its content of ZnTiO3 and TiO2 particles, which act as photocatalytic agents. The MB photodegradation mechanism involving GTA (Fig. 17 ) indicated that exposing the material to UV light excited the valence electrons of the ZnTiO3 and TiO2 photocatalysts to higher energy states. The resulting excited electrons can be transported to the adsorbed methylene blue, leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radicals (HO●) and superoxide (
O 2 −
●). Finally, these highly reactive species attack MB dye molecules, breaking their bonds and oxidizing their functional groups, leading to their decomposition and mineralization [125].

On the other hand, Sarkar et al. tested the removal of Congo red dye under visible-light irradiation by a new photocatalytic composite, specifically an LD slag-based geopolymer doped with zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO/LDSGP) formulated by the hydrothermal method [54]. Significant CR dye degradation of around 97 % was achieved at the initial CR concentration of 5 mg/L in 200 mL at a pH of 6 after 60 min exposure to visible irradiation using 0.2 g ZnO/LDSGP catalyst, which showed excellent catalytic activity. Likewise, Fig. 18 describes the possible mechanism by which the ZnO/LDSGP geopolymer photocatalyst photodegrades the CR dye. Exposure of the ZnO/LDSGP photocatalyst surface to a visible light emission (hν) excited it, causing photogenerated electrons (e-) to migrate from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), overlapping the ZnO/LDSGP bandgap. Next, free electrons react with dissolved oxygen (O2) in the conduction band (CB) to create free oxygen radicals (
O 2 −
●). Meanwhile, water molecules oxidize in the valence band (VB) by coming into contact with holes (h+), leading to the formation of free hydroxyl radicals (HO●). These radicals (
O 2 −
● and HO●) are responsible for the photo-oxidating CR dye to the end products, i.e., carbon dioxide and water.nergy (Eg = 2.95 eV, thus producing H+ hols in the valence band (VB).

Despite different photocatalytic operating conditions or geopolymeric catalyst synthesis methods, most of these semiconductor nanoparticle-doped geopolymeric photocatalysts have shown excellent performance in degrading various organic dyes under visible and ultraviolet light, making them a promising solution for the ultimate removal of aqueous organic dyes by photocatalytic processes.

The photodegradation process targeted pharmaceutical pollutants, covering antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin, tetracycline, 5-fluorouracil, and others, has also seen significant progress with using photocatalytic geopolymers doped by semiconducting nanoparticles. For instance, Cai et al. investigated the performance of an amorphous MnO₂-decorated α-Fe2O3@ geopolymer sphere Z-scheme heterojunction (A-MnO2/Fe2O3@GS) created by the ion-exchange method on the photodegradation of the antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP) under UV light. This study showed that the MnO2/α-Fe₂O₃@geopolymer composite was an excellent photocatalyst for degrading the CIP pollutant. The 2 g/L aforementioned catalyst dose resulted in a significant 99.1 % degradation rate of CIP at a 20-ppm initial concentration and solution pH of 5 after exposure to visible light irradiation for just 40 min [126]. As shown by the proposed photocatalytic mechanism (Fig. 19 ), exposure of A-MnO2/Fe2O3@GS to visible light irradiation excites both MnO2 and Fe2O3@GS, generating e- in CB and h+ in VB. H+ in VB may react with hydroxyl ions to generate OH, given the more positive VB edge of Fe2O3@GS (2.15 eV) than the oxidation potential of OH-/HO● (1.99 eV). Alternatively, the CB edge of MnO2 (-1.01 eV), which is more negative than O2/
O 2 −
● (-0.33 eV), can convert O2 to
O 2 −
●. Synergetic effects between MnO2 and α-Fe2O3 permit photo-generated e- in the CB of Fe2O3@GS to move to the VB of MnO2 via the difference in potential for recombination with photo-generated h+ in MnO2. Moreover, the plentiful surface defects in MnO2 may catch the e- in the CB of MnO2 and equally enrich the O2 for generating additional
O 2 −
● on the surface. Finally, the principal active species (H+,
O 2 −
●, and HO●) degrade the CIP and convert it into intermediates, which themselves decompose into CO2, H2O, and other mineral ions.

In another project carried out by Abukhadra et al., a synthetic kaolinite-based geopolymer matrix was doped with nickel oxide nanoparticles (NiO/GP) through an impregnation technique and used as a catalyst in the decontamination of 5-fluorouracil (5-Fu) drug residues by photocatalytic oxidation [69]. In this case, using 0.1 g/L NiO/GP catalyst was sufficient for the complete oxidation of 5-Fu with an initial concentration of 50 mg/L at a pH of 8 after just 20 min under visible light, while complete mineralization occurred after 100 min. NiO/GP also exhibits remarkable recycling performance, reaching 90.7 % after five reuse cycles. The physical and chemical properties of NiO/GP promote the efficiency of the 5-Fu molecules adsorbed on its surface, bringing them closer to the catalyst-generated oxidizing species. Under visible light illumination, NiO/GPs particles form photogenerated electrons (eCB-) and electron holes (hVB+). After H2O2 decomposition, the molecules H2O2 and O2 reacted with eCB- to produce (HO●) and (
O 2 −
●) radicals. Other hydroxyl radicals (HO●) result when OH- reacts with hVB+. Oxidizing species (HO●) and (
O 2 −
●) contribute to the total decomposition of the 5-Fu pollutant (Fig. 20 ).

Ren et al. studied the degradation performance of a tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) drug using a newly formulated hydrothermally photocatalytic geopolymer (TTG/ZnS) based on alkali-activated tourmaline tail (TTG) and loaded with zinc sulfide nanoparticles (ZnS) [76]. As a result, the rate of TC degradation by TTG/ZnS was found to be good, reaching 91.5 %, using the optimum operating conditions of an initial TC concentration of 10 ppm, a catalyst dosage of 0.5 g/L, pH 6, and 90 min under UV light. As shown in Fig. 21 , it is clear that photodegradation of the TC antibiotic in solution by ZnO/GASG catalyst resulted from the photogenerated h+ and
O 2 −
● resulting from the reduction of O2 adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface as a highly oxidizing active species.

Nanoparticle-modified geopolymers, formulated by different methods and tested under various photocatalytic conditions, have also shown effective catalytic activity, whether exposed to visible or ultraviolet radiation, in decomposing pharmaceutical pollutants, particularly antibiotics, transforming them into green composites.

Recently, nanoparticle-infused geopolymers have been applied as photocatalysts in the degradation of aquatic pesticides such as imidacloprid, a common pollutant found in aquatic environments [127]. Sanguanpak et al. have investigated the use of titanium dioxide-immobilized geopolymer granules (TiO2-GCs) as a new type of photocatalytic geopolymer in degrading the imidacloprid pesticide (IMI) in aqueous media. The results demonstrate that the geopolymer with a maximum TiO2 content of 14 % showed the highest efficiency in imidacloprid degradation of 96.85 % when exposed to UV light for 24 h, particularly after the addition of 20 mM H2O2 with 10 g/L of 14 %-TiO2-GCs to the IMI solution of initial concentration 15 mg/L and pH 9 [128]. Fig. 22 describes the mechanism of imidacloprid photodegradation by the TiO2-immobilized porous geopolymer granule catalyst involving three steps. First, UV radiation excites the TiO2 particles deposited on the geopolymer surface, generating conduction-band e- and valence-band h+. Then, the photo-creating e- reacts with O2 to produce
O 2 −
● radicals, and the photo-creating h+ reacts with H2O to produce HO● radicals. In parallel,
O 2 −
● radicals react with h+ and e- to generate HO● radicals. Finally, HO● and
O 2 −
● radicals attack imidacloprid molecules adsorbed on the geopolymer catalyst for their degradation and conversion into small molecules, including CO2, H2O, NH4 +, NO3 -, and Cl-.

In the same way as the previous study, Peturai et al. also simulated the degradation of imidacloprid under UV irradiation using two types of porous metakaolin-based geopolymer granules, one immobilizing titanium dioxide (TiO2) and the other iron oxide (Fe2O3), as photocatalysts [129]. Under the same operating conditions, the 20 % Fe2O3 immobilized porous geopolymer catalyst was able to completely degrade imidacloprid after just 150 min under UV light, while the 14 % TiO2 immobilized porous geopolymer required 1440 min to achieve a degradation efficiency of 96.6 %. This difference was explained by the possibility of forming more catalytic active sites due to the occurrence of a good and more homogeneous distribution of Fe2O3 in the porous geopolymer structure when Fe3+ was incorporated into its matrix, leading to improved photodegradation of imidacloprid by using the Fe2O3-immobilized geopolymer as described in Fig. 23 . Li et al. proposed a possible mechanism whereby the photo-Fenton reaction in Fe2O3-modified-non-woven systems induces the spontaneous generation of H2O2, which promotes the accelerated formation of HO● radicals [130]. The potential mechanism of imidacloprid photodegradation by the 20 % Fe2O3 immobilized porous geopolymer catalyst shown in Fig. 23 was similarly described to that of the 14 % TiO2 immobilized porous geopolymer catalyst in degrading aquatic IMI pollutant (Fig. 22).

The application of nanoparticle-impregnated geopolymers as photocatalysts in pesticide degradation is still under investigation due to the paucity of published literature concerning this subject. However, it is possible to conclude from the work discussed above that photocatalyst geopolymers have shown good catalytic performance in degrading pesticide pollutants despite employing operational conditions that appear uneconomical, such as high catalyst dosage and long exposure time to the light source. Overcoming these challenges calls for further research into new semiconducting nanoparticles capable of enhancing the catalytic activity of modified geopolymer systems towards the decomposition of various types of organic pollutants on the one hand and optimizing operating conditions on the other.

The choice of semiconductor is not the only factor influencing the performance of photocatalytic geopolymers in the degradation of organic pollutants. The composition, particle size, specific surface area, concentration, and type of photocatalyst have a fundamental influence on the performance of the decomposition of organic molecules. The reduction in particle size and increase in the specific surface area of the photocatalyst lead to significant degradation results. In contrast, the geopolymer surface functional groups improve the mechanical properties, stability, dispersion, and photocatalyst adhesion [131]. Consequently, the intensity and light source (sunlight, UV light, etc.) should correspond to the absorption spectrum of the selected photocatalyst. The surface charge of the pollutants and the photocatalyst depends on the solution's pH, which directly influences the yield of the photodegradation process. Additionally, the high temperature and long reaction time can improve doped geopolymers' photocatalytic activity until deactivation occurs due to thermal stability restrictions. It is necessary to establish the equilibrium between the diverse factors cited above to ensure the maximum effectiveness in the degradation of organic pollutants using photocatalytic doped geopolymers.

Overall, the advancements in nanoparticle-doped geopolymers for photodegradation showcase significant potential for improving water quality and protecting aquatic environments. This comparative analysis helps identify the most suitable doped geopolymers for various environmental applications, aiding researchers and practitioners in selecting the optimal materials for specific water treatment challenges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions, current limitations, and future perspectives

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle-doped geopolymers have demonstrated excellent potential as photocatalysts for degrading organic pollutants, particularly dyes, pharmaceuticals, and pesticides. Their ability to combine the structural stability of geopolymers with the high photocatalytic performance of semiconductor nanoparticles makes them attractive for sustainable water treatment applications. Various synthesis methods, including direct mixing, ion exchange, dip-coating, and hydrothermal techniques, influence their physicochemical properties and overall efficiency. Structural and optical characterizations have provided valuable insights into their morphology, porosity, and electronic behavior, allowing for enhanced material design. Studies have shown that these materials exhibit high degradation efficiency under ultraviolet and solar irradiation, reinforcing their potential for large-scale environmental applications. However, despite significant advancements in synthesis and application, many restrictions need to be addressed to achieve full practical implementation in wastewater treatment.

Several challenges hinder the widespread application of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers in real-world water treatment systems. One major limitation is stability and recyclability, as repeated photocatalytic cycles may lead to structural degradation, nanoparticle leaching, or reduced efficiency over time. Additionally, material consistency and standardization remain problematic, as variations in synthesis protocols often lead to inconsistent photocatalytic performance, making large-scale production difficult. Scalability and economic feasibility also pose challenges, as most research is conducted at a laboratory scale under controlled conditions that do not fully replicate the complexities of industrial wastewater treatment. Furthermore, the environmental fate of degradation byproducts remains largely unexplored, raising concerns about the potential toxicity and secondary pollution that may arise from incomplete pollutant degradation. Finally, environmental factors such as pH fluctuations, competing contaminants, and water composition can influence the performance of these photocatalysts, requiring further studies to assess their behavior under diverse operating conditions .

Future research should focus on improving the stability and recyclability of nanoparticle-doped geopolymers by developing more robust composite structures, enhancing nanoparticle immobilization, and incorporating regeneration strategies to maintain long-term activity. Optimizing synthesis methods to achieve uniform nanoparticle dispersion and controlled doping concentrations will be crucial for ensuring reproducible and scalable production. Large-scale pilot studies should be conducted to evaluate their performance under real wastewater treatment conditions, addressing factors such as flow rate, pollutant mixtures, and treatment efficiency over extended periods. Additionally, exploring hybrid treatment approaches that combine photocatalysis with adsorption, membrane filtration, or advanced oxidation processes could further enhance pollutant removal efficiency. A detailed assessment of degradation byproducts and their environmental impact should be prioritized to ensure that photocatalytic treatment does not introduce new ecological risks. Integrating multi-functional geopolymers with antibacterial, self-cleaning, or selective adsorption properties may also open new opportunities for their application in broader environmental contexts. Advancing these materials through interdisciplinary collaboration between material science, environmental engineering, and industrial stakeholders will be essential for transitioning from laboratory research to full-scale implementation. By addressing these challenges and research gaps, nanoparticle-doped geopolymers can become a key component in the next generation of cost-effective, sustainable, and scalable water treatment technologies.
